  • 有斩罪三 - Refers to having three capital offenses, a serious accusation.
  • 富于春秋 - Means 'young and inexperienced,' often used to describe a young ruler.
  • 积思于六经 - Means 'to deeply contemplate the Six Classics,' referring to the foundational texts of Confucianism.
  • 留神于王事 - Means 'to pay close attention to state affairs.'
  • 驰骛于唐虞 - Refers to emulating the virtues of the legendary rulers Tang and Yu, who were known for their wise governance.
  • 折节于三代 - Means 'to humble oneself and learn from the Three Dynasties' (Xia, Shang, and Zhou), emphasizing the importance of historical precedent.
  • 靡丽为右 - Means 'to favor luxury and beauty,' indicating a preference for superficial and materialistic values.
  • 奢侈为务 - Means 'to make luxury a primary concern,' highlighting the pursuit of excessive wealth and enjoyment.
  • 尽狗马之乐 - Means 'to indulge in the pleasures of dogs and horses,' referring to the enjoyment of hunting and other recreational activities.
  • 极耳目之欲 - Means 'to satisfy every sensory desire,' indicating a life of indulgence.
  • 行邪枉之道 - Means 'to follow crooked and unjust paths,' suggesting immoral behavior.
  • 经淫僻之路 - Means 'to travel on the path of debauchery and perversion,' further emphasizing immoral and decadent behavior.
  • 国家之大贼 - Means 'a great enemy of the state,' indicating someone who poses a significant threat to the nation.
  • 人主之大蜮 - Means 'a great pest to the ruler,' suggesting a significant threat to the emperor.
  • 淫首 - Means 'leader of debauchery,' indicating the person who leads others into immoral behavior.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 东方朔 - A famous court jester during the Han Dynasty, known for his wit and wisdom.
  • 董偃 - A historical figure who was accused of improper behavior with the princess.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics, which are the foundational texts of Confucianism, including the *Book of Changes* (Yijing), *Book of Documents* (Shujing), *Book of Songs* (Shijing), *Book of Rites* (Liji), *Book of History* (Shi), and *Spring and Autumn Annals* (Chunqiu).
  • 唐虞 - The legendary rulers Tang and Yu, who were known for their wise governance and moral integrity.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties, which are often cited as models of good governance and moral leadership.
  • 靡丽 - Refers to a preference for luxury and beauty, often associated with superficial and materialistic values.
  • 奢侈 - Refers to excessive wealth and enjoyment, often seen as a vice.
  • 狗马之乐 - Refers to the enjoyment of hunting and other recreational activities, often associated with the nobility.
  • 耳目之欲 - Refers to the satisfaction of sensory desires, often seen as a form of indulgence.
  • 邪枉 - Refers to crooked and unjust paths, indicating immoral behavior.
  • 淫僻 - Refers to debauchery and perversion, further emphasizing immoral and decadent behavior.
  • 国家之大贼 - Refers to a great enemy of the state, indicating someone who poses a significant threat to the nation.
  • 人主之大蜮 - Refers to a great pest to the ruler, suggesting a significant threat to the emperor.
  • 淫首 - Refers to a leader of debauchery, indicating the person who leads others into immoral behavior.

Proper Nouns

  • 东方朔 - A historical figure known for his wit and wisdom.
  • 董偃 - A historical figure who was accused of improper behavior with the princess.
  • 六经 - The Six Classics, which are the foundational texts of Confucianism.
  • 唐虞 - The legendary rulers Tang and Yu.
  • 三代 - The Xia, Shang, and Zhou dynasties.
  • 陛下 - An honorific address to the emperor.

  • 宣室者，先帝之正处也 - Statement meaning 'The Xuan Room is the proper place of the late emperor.'
  • 非法度之政，不得入焉 - Statement meaning 'Without the authority of law and regulation, one cannot enter.'
  • 放淫乱之渐，其变为篡 - Statement meaning 'The gradual spread of debauchery will turn into usurpation.'
  • 竖貂为淫，而易牙作患 - Statement meaning 'Shudi was debauched, and Yiyá caused trouble.'
  • 庆父诛，而鲁国全；管蔡诛，而周室安 - Statement meaning 'When Qingfu was executed, the state of Lu was preserved; when Guan and Cai were executed, the Zhou dynasty was stabilized.'
  • 有诏止，更置酒北宫 - Statement meaning 'There was an imperial edict to stop, and instead, wine was served in the northern palace.'
  • 引董君从东司马门 - Statement meaning 'Dong Jun was led out through the eastern司马门.'

  • 赐朔黄金三十斤 - Statement meaning 'Shuo was given thirty catties of gold.'
  • 董君之宠，由是日衰 - Statement meaning 'Dong Jun's favor gradually declined.'
  • 至年三十而终 - Statement meaning 'He died at the age of thirty.'
  • 后数岁，窦太主卒，与董君合葬于霸陵 - Statement meaning 'After several years, Princess Dou died, and she was buried with Dong Jun in the Baling tomb.'
  • 是后公主贵人多逾礼制，自董偃始 - Statement meaning 'After this, many princesses and noblewomen exceeded the proper etiquette, starting with Dong Yan.'

The phrase means 'but if you do not reveal this to others for a hundred days, you will attain immortality and reach the highest rank in the imperial court.'
  • 三子复拜谢 - '复' (fù) means 'again,' and '拜谢' (bài xiè) means 'to bow and thank.' The phrase means 'the three sons bowed and thanked her again.'
  • 但以愚昧为忧 - '但' (dàn) means 'but' or 'only,' '愚昧' (yú mèi) means 'ignorant' or 'foolish,' and '为忧' (wéi yōu) means 'to worry.' The phrase means 'but they worried about their ignorance.'
  • 易耳 - A colloquial expression meaning 'easy' or 'simple.'
  • 乃敕地上主者，令召孔宣父 - '敕' (chì) means 'to order,' '地上主者' (dì shàng zhǔ zhě) means 'the earthly master' or 'the earthly ruler,' and '令召' (lìng zhào) means 'to summon.' The phrase means 'she then ordered the earthly ruler to summon Confucius.'
  • 须臾，孔子具冠剑而至 - '须臾' (xū yú) means 'in a moment,' '具' (jù) means 'prepared,' '冠剑' (guān jiàn) refers to Confucius's ceremonial attire, and '而至' (ér zhì) means 'and arrived.' The phrase means 'in a moment, Confucius arrived, prepared in ceremonial attire.'
  • 夫人临阶，宣父拜谒甚恭 - '临阶' (lín jiē) means 'to approach the steps,' '拜谒' (bài yè) means 'to pay respects,' and '甚恭' (shèn gōng) means 'very respectfully.' The phrase means 'the lady approached the steps, and Confucius paid respects very respectfully.'
  • 夫人端立，微劳问之 - '端立' (duān lì) means 'to stand upright,' and '微劳问之' (wēi láo wèn zhī) means 'to gently inquire.' The phrase means 'the lady stood upright and gently inquired.'
  • 指六籍篇目以示之 - '指' (zhǐ) means 'to point to,' '六籍' (liù jí) refers to the Six Classics, '篇目' (piān mù) means 'titles of chapters,' and '以示之' (yǐ shì zhī) means 'to show them.' The phrase means 'he pointed to the titles of chapters in the Six Classics and showed them.'
  • 莫不了然解悟，大义悉通，咸若素习 - '莫不' (mò bù) means 'all,' '了然解悟' (liǎo rán jiě wù) means 'fully understand and comprehend,' '大义' (dà yì) means 'major principles,' '悉通' (xī tōng) means 'all understood,' and '咸若素习' (xián ruò sù xí) means 'as if they had always practiced it.' The phrase means 'all of them fully understood and comprehended the major principles, as if they had always practiced it.'
  • 既而宣父谢去 - '既而' (jì ér) means 'afterward,' '谢去' (xiè qù) means 'to take leave.' The phrase means 'afterward, Confucius took his leave.'
  • 夫人又命周尚云，示以玄女符、玉璜秘诀 - '命' (mìng) means 'to order,' '周尚云' (zhōu shàng yún) is a person's name, '示以' (shì yǐ) means 'to show,' '玄女符' (xuán nǚ fú) refers to talismans associated with the Queen Mother of the West, and '玉璜秘诀' (yù huáng mì jué) means 'secrets of the jade璜.' The phrase means 'the lady then ordered Zhou Shangyun to show them the talismans of the Queen Mother of the West and the secrets of the jade璜.'
  • 三子又得之无遗 - '得之无遗' (dé zhī wú yí) means 'obtained without omission.' The phrase means 'the three sons obtained everything without omission.'
  • 复坐与言 - '复坐' (fù zuò) means 'sat down again,' and '与言' (yǔ yán) means 'to talk with.' The phrase means 'they sat down again and talked.'
  • 则皆文武全才，学究天人之际矣 - '则皆' (zé jiē) means 'then all,' '文武全才' (wén wǔ quán cái) means 'fully talented in both literature and martial arts,' '学究天人之际' (xué jiū tiān rén zhī jì) means 'studied the relationship between heaven and humanity.' The phrase means 'then all of them were fully talented in both literature and martial arts, having studied the relationship between heaven and humanity.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 六籍 - Refers to the Six Classics, which are foundational texts in Confucianism, including the Book of Changes (Yi Jing), the Book of Documents (Shu Jing), the Book of Songs (Shi Jing), the Book of Rites (Li Ji), the Book of Music (Yue Jing), and the Spring and Autumn Annals (Chun Qiu).
  • 玄女符 - Talismans associated with the Queen Mother of the West (Xuan Nu), a figure in Chinese mythology often depicted as a powerful and wise female deity.
  • 玉璜 - A type of jade ornament, often used in ancient Chinese rituals and ceremonies, symbolizing purity and nobility.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • The sequence '少简贵，有清标，姿度美秀，善谈论，工草隶' describes Guo Han's character and abilities, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '早孤，独处，乘月卧庭中' describes Guo Han's background and daily routine, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '时时有微风，稍闻香气渐浓' describes the environmental changes, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '翰甚怪之，仰视空中，见有人冉冉而下，直至翰前，乃一少女也' describes Guo Han's reaction and the appearance of the girl, and must be read as a single narrative clause.
  • The sequence '明艳绝代，光彩溢目，衣玄绡之衣，曳罗霜之帔，戴翠翘凤凰之冠，蹑琼文九章之履' describes the girl's appearance, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '侍女二人，皆有殊色，感荡心神' describes the maidservants, and must be read as a single descriptive clause.
  • The sequence '翰整衣巾下床拜谒，不意尊灵迥降，愿垂德音' describes Guo Han's respectful gesture and his request, and must be read as a single narrative clause.
  • The sequence '吾天上织女也，久无主对，而嘉期阻旷，幽态盈怀，上帝赐命而游人间' describes the girl's identity and her reasons for visiting the mortal world, and must be read as a single narrative clause.

Syntactic Ambiguities

  • This sequence describes the old woman taking out the medicine and the man immediately beginning to grind it, working during the day and resting at night.
  • This clause describes the old woman taking the mortar and pestle into the inner chamber at night.
  • This sequence describes the man hearing the sound of grinding medicine again and looking to see a jade rabbit holding a mortar and pestle, with the moonlight illuminating the room brightly.
  • This clause describes the old woman taking the medicine and swallowing it, saying she will go into the cave to inform her relatives and prepare a tent for Pai Lang.
  • This sequence describes the old woman taking the woman into the mountain and telling the man to wait a moment, followed by carriages, servants, and attendants welcoming the man.
  • This clause describes the man seeing a grand mansion that reaches the clouds, with pearl doors shining under the sun.
  • This clause describes the interior of the mansion, with luxurious furnishings and treasures, resembling the home of a noble family.
  • This clause describes the man being led into the tent by fairy children and servants, and the ceremony being completed.

  • 君未列仙品，不合正御，故不下也 - Means 'You are not yet listed among the immortals, so you are not fit to be the main one, hence they do not descend.' '君未列仙品' means 'You are not yet listed among the immortals,' '不合正御' means 'so you are not fit to be the main one,' and '故不下也' means 'hence they do not descend.'
  • 声韵清锵，奏讫而云雾霏然，已不见矣 - Means 'the sound was clear and resonant, and after the performance, clouds and mist descended, and they were no longer seen.' '声韵清锵' means 'the sound was clear and resonant,' '奏讫' means 'after the performance,' '云雾霏然' means 'clouds and mist descended,' and '已不见矣' means 'and they were no longer seen.'

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 唯卿所欲 - Formal expression meaning 'whatever you desire,' often used in polite or deferential contexts.
  • 不难致 - Means 'not difficult to obtain' or 'easily acquired.'
  • 忧卿福薄 - Literally 'worry about your thin fortune,' meaning 'concerned about your limited blessings' or 'fear that your good fortune is insufficient.'
  • 温婉 - Describes speech or behavior as gentle, polite, and graceful.
  • 款洽 - Means 'cordial' or 'warmly friendly,' often used to describe social interactions.
  • 唯唯 - A formal response indicating agreement or acquiescence, often used to show politeness.
  • 不能出一言 - Means 'unable to speak a word,' often used to describe a state of silence or inability to respond.
  • 薄晓丝竹 - Literally 'slightly know the sounds of strings and pipes,' meaning 'have a slight understanding of music.'
  • 清越 - Describes a sound as clear and resonant, often used for musical tones.
  • 按也 - Means 'to follow' or 'to play according to a score,' often used in the context of music.
  • 下俚调 - Refers to lowbrow or popular tunes, often in contrast to more refined or classical music.
  • 凤凰来 - A metaphor for the arrival of a virtuous person or a sign of good fortune.
  • 不羞为徐郎作奴 - Literally 'not ashamed to be a servant of Xu Lang,' meaning 'not ashamed to serve someone of higher status or talent.'

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 卿 - A term of address used in classical Chinese, often for a close friend, subordinate, or someone of lower status.
  • 丝竹 - Refers to string and wind instruments, often used to denote music in general.
  • 箫 - A type of bamboo flute, a common wind instrument in ancient China.
  • 美人 - A term for a beautiful woman, often used in classical literature to describe a virtuous or talented woman.
  • 徐郎 - A reference to Xu Xuan, a famous musician and poet from the Tang Dynasty, known for his musical talent and poetic skill.
  • 逡巡 - Means 'to hesitate' or 'to delay,' often used to describe a moment of pause or hesitation.
  • 侍女 - Refers to a female servant or attendant, often used in classical literature to describe the attendants of noblewomen or empresses.
  • 榻 - A low bed or couch, often used in classical Chinese settings for reclining or sleeping.

Proper Nouns

  • 鏊 - The name of the person being addressed, likely a historical or literary figure.
  • 夫人 - A title for a noblewoman or empress, often used in classical literature.
  • 美人 - A term for a beautiful woman, often used in classical literature to describe a virtuous or talented woman.

Idiomatic Expressions

  • 帏帐茵席 - Refers to luxurious bedding and curtains, emphasizing the opulence of the setting.
  • 穷极瑰丽 - Means 'extremely beautiful and magnificent,' describing the grandeur of the surroundings.
  • 非复鏊向时之比也 - Means 'not comparable to the time of Aò,' indicating a significant change in circumstances.
  • 试诈跌入地 - Means 'try to pretend to fall to the ground,' suggesting a deliberate action to test a reaction.
  • 榻下已遍铺锦褥 - Means 'the floor under the bed was already covered with brocade cushions,' describing the meticulous preparation.
  • 殆无隙地 - Means 'there was hardly any empty space,' emphasizing the thoroughness of the setup.
  • 美人解衣，独着红绡裹肚一事 - Means 'the beautiful woman undressed, wearing only a red gauze wrap,' describing her attire.
  • 相与就枕，交会 - Means 'they went to bed together and engaged in intimate relations,' describing the intimate act.
  • 已而流丹浃籍 - Means 'soon after, there was a flow of blood and fluid,' describing the aftermath of the act.
  • 宛转恇怯难胜 - Means 'she was weak and timid, unable to bear it,' describing her physical and emotional state.
  • 情志飞荡，颠倒若狂 - Means 'his emotions were soaring, and he was completely disoriented,' describing his emotional state.
  • 竟莫能一言 - Means 'he could not say a word,' describing his silence.
  • 天且明 - Means 'it was almost dawn,' indicating the time of day.
  • 侍女十馀 - Means 'more than ten maidservants,' describing the number of attendants.
  • 奉匜沃盥 - Means 'they brought water and washed,' describing the ritual of washing.
  • 良久，妆讫言别 - Means 'after a long while, she finished dressing and said goodbye,' describing the end of the encounter.
  • 时运相从，良非容易 - Means 'it is not easy to be together by fate,' expressing the rarity of their meeting.
  • 此后欢好无间 - Means 'from now on, we will be inseparable in joy,' expressing a commitment to continued intimacy.
  • 卿举一念，身即却来 - Means 'if you think of me, I will come back,' expressing a promise of mutual availability.
  • 但忧卿意不坚 - Means 'I only worry that your intention is not firm,' expressing concern about his loyalty.
  • 或轻向人道，不为卿福耳 - Means 'or if you lightly tell others about it, it will not be good for you,' expressing a warning about discretion.

Cultural and Historical Allusions

  • 帏帐茵席 - Refers to luxurious bedding and curtains, which were common in ancient Chinese literature to describe opulent settings.
  • 美人 - Refers to a beautiful woman, often used in classical Chinese literature to describe a lover or concubine.
  • 红绡裹肚 - Refers to a red gauze wrap, a type of undergarment worn by women in ancient China.
  • 侍女 - Refers to maidservants, who were part of the household staff in ancient China.
  • 奉匜沃盥 - Refers to the ritual of washing, a common practice in ancient Chinese culture.

Proper Nouns

  • 鏊 - The name of the protagonist, a man in the story.
  • 美人 - The name of the beautiful woman, a concubine or lover.
